title: Park Safety

jokes.text: No, they don't win a hummingbird. I installed hummingbird feeders in all the parks, so the winner gets to refill those feeders.
jokes.setup: The name 'hummingbird lottery' suggests people win actual hummingbirds
jokes.punchline: They just win the chore of refilling feeders
jokes.explanation: Classic Leslie moment - turning mundane tasks into elaborate systems. Good misdirection with efficient setup/payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: They just win the chore of refilling feeders

jokes.text: Scientifically, hummingbirds are the world's cutest animals. I mean, they're so small. And they have tiny beaks. And they only eat sugar water. I mean, what beats that?
jokes.setup: Leslie's earnest scientific declaration about hummingbirds being cutest
jokes.punchline: Her reasoning is childlike enthusiasm rather than actual science
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - mixing pseudo-scientific authority with pure enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hummingbirds are scientifically the cutest animal. It's been proven. Their little wings go so fast they're basically just a blur of adorableness, and their beaks are like tiny swords of cuteness.

jokes.text: Come on. Baby monkeys in diapers? Yeah. They do.
jokes.setup: Someone off-camera challenges her hummingbird claim
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately concedes when faced with baby monkeys in diapers
jokes.explanation: Great character beat - Leslie's honesty trumps her enthusiasm. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. Baby monkeys in diapers. You win.

jokes.text: Not me. Not me. Not me. Not me. Jerry. Yeah!
jokes.setup: Everyone tries to avoid the hummingbird duty
jokes.punchline: Jerry gets stuck with it again
jokes.explanation: Sets up Jerry as the office punching bag, functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, we need someone to handle the hummingbird feeders this week.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Not me. I'm allergic to nature.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'd rather eat glass.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Child, please.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't do birds. They're plotting something.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I would, but I have that... thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll do it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Really? That's so nice of you, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I always seem to—

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: Great, thanks Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: —end up with the worst jobs anyway.

jokes.text: This is my third time in a row. Just a bad luck streak, buddy. Next time, I'm sure it will definitely be one of us. But it won't be me. Because I always write...
jokes.setup: Tom pretends to console Jerry about bad luck
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals he's actively avoiding selection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Tom's self-serving nature, solid structure

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey Jerry, I heard about your bad luck. That's rough, man. I'm really sorry to hear that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Thanks, Tom. I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, no problem. Hey, you know what? I'm gonna go stand over there. Far away from you. Because I don't want any of that bad luck rubbing off on me.

jokes.text: No, I always write my own name. But just to be safe, I do add 20 extra 'Jerry's.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining his lottery strategy
jokes.punchline: He sabotages himself by adding extra entries
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry moment - his kindness toward others leads to self-sabotage. Great character integration and absurd logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have a system. I only play numbers that have never won before.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That way, they're due. It's basic math.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I bought 500 extra tickets with those numbers to increase my odds.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Then I realized I didn't want to win because everyone would know I'm an idiot.

jokes.text: I thought Freddy Spaghetti OD'd. No. That's Mr. Funny Noodle. And he didn't OD, his drummer shot him.
jokes.setup: Casual mention of children's performer overdosing
jokes.punchline: Correction reveals an even darker fate for a different pasta-themed entertainer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. The casual delivery of increasingly dark children's entertainer fates is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Did you hear about that children's entertainer who overdosed?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, no. Who?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Actually, I was wrong. It wasn't an overdose. It was Alfredo.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Alfredo the puppet. He drowned in a vat of his own sauce.

jokes.text: Maybe he fell into the toilet. Remember when he fell into the toilet?
jokes.setup: Wondering where Jerry is
jokes.punchline: Reference to Jerry's past toilet mishap
jokes.explanation: Builds Jerry's history of accidents, April's deadpan delivery helps

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Where's Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Probably in the bathroom. Again.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What is it with him and toilets?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't ask questions I don't want answered.

jokes.text: Oh, sorry, guys. Sorry I'm late. I got confused and took a shower, after I got dressed, because I'm Jerry.
jokes.setup: Tom doing an impression of Jerry's excuses
jokes.punchline: The absurd logic of showering after getting dressed
jokes.explanation: Good character work from Tom, the Jerry impression captures his confused helplessness

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I can't go to the meeting, I just got dressed.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm gonna shower now.

jokes.text: A 'schlemiel' is the guy who spills soup at a fancy party. A 'schlimazel' is the guy he spills it on. Jerry is both the 'schlemiel' and the 'schlimazel' of our office.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining Yiddish terms for unlucky people
jokes.punchline: Jerry manages to be both the cause and victim of bad luck
jokes.explanation: Excellent cultural reference perfectly applied to Jerry's character. Great structure and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: A schlemiel is someone who's always getting into accidents. A schlimazel is someone who always has bad luck. And Jerry? Jerry is both.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: He's a schlemiel-schlimazel. He trips and falls into a hole, and somehow the hole was already there because of something he did last week.

jokes.text: And then I put my underwear on my head instead of my butt.
jokes.setup: Continuing the Jerry impression game
jokes.punchline: Absurd confusion about basic clothing
jokes.explanation: Relies on 'random equals funny' but has some charm in the delivery

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. So you're telling me that the thing you wear on your feet is called a 'shoe'? Like, one shoe?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, Tom, that's what a shoe is.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: But then what do you call it when you have two of them?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Shoes. Plural.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's the dumbest thing I've ever heard. Why would you make the plural the same as the singular? That's not how English works.

jokes.text: Okay. Okay, guys. That's enough. Unless somebody has another good one.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to stop the Jerry mockery
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermines her own attempt to protect him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her people-pleasing nature conflicts with her protective instincts

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, I need to say something. Jerry is a valued member of this department, and I think we should all show him some respect.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He works hard, he's dedicated, and frankly, it's not his fault that he's a walking disaster.

jokes.text: Jerry, are you okay? Ann, is Jerry okay? What's wrong with your arm? Ann, tell me what's wrong with Jerry's arm. Jerry, talk to me. Ann, get Jerry to talk to me.
jokes.setup: Leslie sees injured Jerry
jokes.punchline: Her panic creates a confusing triangle of communication
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie panic behavior, the repetitive structure builds the comedy

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Jerry, are you okay? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm fine, I just tripped on the—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben! BEN! Call an ambulance! Jerry's dying!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, he's standing up.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: He's in shock, Ben! Jerry, don't listen to him, you're in shock!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I really am okay—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And now he's in denial! This is worse than I thought!

jokes.text: Why? Did you throw out your shoulder trying to swing a honey pot off your hand?
jokes.setup: Jerry says people will laugh at what happened
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate absurd guess about Jerry's accident
jokes.explanation: Funny specific absurd scenario that feels Jerry-like, good character work from Tom

jokes.text: Ew! Is that code for some kind of weird sex act? Lord Sheldon is my dog. My wife named him. Ew!
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions walking 'Lord Sheldon'
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate assumption it's sexual, followed by Jerry's innocent explanation and Tom's continued disgust
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Tom's suspicious mind vs Jerry's innocence

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Lord Sheldon? What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's my dog.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh my God. I don't want to hear about that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? He's a rescue. I named him after Sheldon Cooper.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Still don't want to hear it.

jokes.text: How did you counter-attack? Fist to the throat? Did you hit him in the beanbag? There's no shame in attacking a criminal's beanbag.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about Jerry's self-defense
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and absurd fighting advice culminating in 'beanbag' euphemism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy character moment - childish enthusiasm for violence with oddly specific terminology

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So Jerry, you know self-defense?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I've taken a few classes...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, so if someone attacks you, what do you do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I... I try to get away.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Right, but like, what if they're coming at you with a knife?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'd run.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What if they're coming at you with a knife AND they're on roller skates?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I... I don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What if they're on roller skates and they're also on fire?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Andy, I don't think—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And they've got like five knives, and they're screaming about burritos? Well, the only way to defend yourself is to grab them by the beanbag.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The beanbag?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, their beanbag.

jokes.text: No, I just curled up and laid still until they left. Well, that's another way to play it.
jokes.setup: Jerry describes his passive response to mugging
jokes.punchline: Andy's diplomatic understatement about Jerry's 'strategy'
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between characters, Andy's genuine attempt to be supportive is funny

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A guy came up to me on the street and said 'Give me your wallet.' So I just gave it to him.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that's one way to handle it.

jokes.text: If even one of them had a unique scar, we got them. I didn't get a good look. Damn it, Jerry. Sorry. You're the victim. Sorry. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to get identifying details from Jerry
jokes.punchline: Her frustration with Jerry overrides her sympathy, leading to immediate guilt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her competence conflicts with her empathy, creating internal comedy

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, I need you to describe the person who hit your car.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, he was tall. Or maybe average height. I'm not great with heights.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, anything else? Hair color?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Could've been brown. Or black. Or possibly blonde. It was hard to tell with the sun.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It was 9 PM, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm sorry. Are you okay? Do you need anything?

jokes.text: Wouldn't it be karma if we were the ones that got mugged? Yeah. That's how pathetic Jerry is. He can't even get karma right.
jokes.setup: Tom questioning if Jerry's mugging is karma for their treatment
jokes.punchline: April's logic that Jerry is so pathetic he gets other people's karma
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic from April. The idea that Jerry is so unlucky he breaks cosmic justice is perfectly cruel and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You think this is karma? For how we treat Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, Jerry's so pathetic that he absorbs other people's karma. He's like a karma sponge. We've been mean to him for years, so he's probably carrying around all of our negative energy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's not how karma works.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Well, nothing about Jerry makes sense, so why should his karma?

jokes.text: Our friend got mugged this morning. And we will not let that happen in vain. He doesn't have a black eye. Well, frankly, the whole department has a black eye.
jokes.setup: Leslie's dramatic speech about not letting the mugging happen in vain
jokes.punchline: Tom's literal interpretation followed by Leslie's metaphorical comeback
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay moment, Leslie's ability to turn Tom's literalism into motivation

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We can't let what happened to me be in vain. This mugging has to mean something. We have to turn this tragedy into action.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, so we're gonna catch the guy who mugged you?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, Tom. Metaphorically. We're going to fight back against crime in this city.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh. Because I was about to say, the police are already doing that.

jokes.text: Jerry's face is the symbol of failure. Our failure to keep the parks safe.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to make Jerry's mugging symbolic
jokes.punchline: Accidentally making Jerry himself the symbol of failure
jokes.explanation: Leslie's good intentions leading to accidentally insulting Jerry is peak character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We need to make this mugging symbolic of something bigger. A cautionary tale about the dangers of Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I appreciate that, Leslie, but I'm just a guy who got mugged.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Exactly! You're the everyman. The symbol of our city's vulnerability.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what does that make me?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're the face of failure, Jerry. The living, breathing emblem of what's wrong with Pawnee.

jokes.text: So, when you say that you want me to check in with the police liaison, you mean hook up with him, right? No. Just check in with him.
jokes.setup: April clarifying Leslie's assignment
jokes.punchline: April's assumption that all tasks involve romantic activity
jokes.explanation: Good April character moment - her deadpan delivery makes the assumption funnier

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So you want me to go through all the old Parks Department files and organize them by date?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, exactly. It's a big project, but I know you can handle it.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: And this doesn't involve me seducing anyone?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No. Why would it?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I don't know. Seemed like all your assignments lately have been about getting people together or going on romantic adventures.

jokes.text: We certainly are a bunch of weaklings. Especially Tom. I am not a weakling. Arm wrestle me right now.
jokes.setup: Ron calling everyone weaklings, specifically Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate challenge that he's about to lose spectacularly
jokes.explanation: Sets up physical comedy, good character work showing Tom's pride vs reality

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You're all weaklings.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm a weakling? I challenge you to an arm-wrestling match right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You're on.

jokes.text: I think I'm more than holding my own here... Three, four, five... Hey. Six. Hey! How you doing? Not too bad.
jokes.setup: Tom claiming to hold his own in arm wrestling
jokes.punchline: Ron counting while casually having a conversation, completely dominating
jokes.explanation: Great physical comedy setup, Ron's casual dominance while chatting is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I can take you. I've been working out.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Alright.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: One. Two. Three.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about getting into woodworking. Four. Five.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Really focus on hand-crafted furniture. Six. Seven.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Eight. Nine. Ten.

jokes.text: The black guy with the Looney Tunes ties? I love him. No. Jerry who works with Leslie.
jokes.setup: Chris thinks Ann means a different Jerry
jokes.punchline: The other Jerry is apparently more memorable and likeable
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke that reinforces our Jerry's forgettable nature

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Wait, which Jerry are we talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Jerry Gergich. From the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Oh! I thought you meant Jerry Garcia.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No, our Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. I just... Jerry Garcia is a lot more popular.

jokes.text: That Jerry? Yeah. He got mugged? Oh. Well, I mean, that's kind of a bummer, too.
jokes.setup: Chris realizes it's the less memorable Jerry
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm immediately deflates to polite disappointment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Chris's honesty about his preferences

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Oh, Jerry.

jokes.text: Scrotation Marks. I don't know what you're talking about, but my gut says no.
jokes.setup: Andy pitching a new band name
jokes.punchline: Ann's immediate rejection based purely on instinct
jokes.explanation: Gross but clever wordplay, Ann's instant rejection is perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What about 'The Awesome Possum'?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: How are people going to become fans if they literally don't know the name of the band they're listening to?
jokes.setup: Andy constantly changing band names
jokes.punchline: Ann pointing out the obvious marketing problem
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man observation about Andy's impractical behavior

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Wait, your band is called Scarecrow Boat? How is that a good name?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? It's awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, nobody's going to search for 'Scarecrow Boat' on the internet.

jokes.text: The problem is, when you're his girlfriend, you're also his mother, and his maid and his nurse. He's completely helpless. He's like a baby in a straightjacket.
jokes.setup: Ann describing her relationship with Andy
jokes.punchline: The specific image of a baby in a straightjacket captures Andy's helpless nature
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphor that perfectly captures Andy's character, well-structured escalation

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy is like a baby in a straightjacket. He doesn't know what's happening, he has no control over the situation, and he's definitely going to hurt himself.

jokes.text: Ooh! 'Baby in a Straightjacket.' That's a good band name. I should tell him that.
jokes.setup: Ann just used the phrase to criticize Andy
jokes.punchline: She immediately considers giving him the very insult as a band name
jokes.explanation: Great ironic moment where Ann's criticism becomes helpful, good callback structure

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You're such a doofus.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Wait, that would be a great band name.

jokes.text: Ha-ha, guys. Really funny. Where's the real banner? We only had an hour. So did I. Look what I did.
jokes.setup: Jerry sees a presumably terrible banner made for him
jokes.punchline: Leslie made a professional banner in the same time, highlighting the others' lack of effort
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Leslie's competence and others' minimal effort

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I made you a banner too. I had the same amount of time as everyone else.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look how professional it is.

jokes.text: Really. My gosh, you should not have gone to all this trouble. Oh. It's no trouble for our buddy.
jokes.setup: Jerry thanking them for the banner effort
jokes.punchline: Tom's response when they clearly did the bare minimum
jokes.explanation: Standard irony joke, functional but not particularly inspired

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Thank you all so much for the banner. It means a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, we wanted to do something special for you, Jerry. You know, really go the extra mile.

jokes.text: He needs a lot of support. Tom. Talking about a bra for a man.
jokes.setup: Leslie saying Jerry needs support
jokes.punchline: Tom's immediate bra joke despite the serious moment
jokes.explanation: Predictable pun but shows Tom's inability to resist a joke even when inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry needs our support right now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, he definitely needs a bra.

jokes.text: Jerry? I don't... I don't think your computer is plugged in. I'm sorry, guys. Just got to power up.
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to do a presentation
jokes.punchline: Basic tech failure that exemplifies his incompetence
jokes.explanation: Standard Jerry incompetence moment, predictable but character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, if I could just get this projector to work...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, you've been standing there for five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, I know, just... there we go!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh no, the laptop's not... it's not connecting.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Did you try turning it off and on again?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know what that means.

jokes.text: You went on a vacation and you chose Muncie, Indiana? Yeah. My wife and I have a time-share. In Muncie? Tom, Muncie is a lovely city.
jokes.setup: Jerry's vacation photo reveals his destination
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of choosing Muncie for vacation, defended by Jerry and Leslie
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Jerry's modest aspirations and Leslie's loyalty to all Indiana cities

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, you went to Muncie, Indiana for vacation?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's a beautiful place!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, Muncie has a rich history. It was a center of American manufacturing and—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Leslie, it's Muncie. There's nothing there.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My wife and I had a wonderful time at the Radisson.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Of course you did.

jokes.text: I said 'twout' instead of 'trout.' It happens to everyone. My marbles are full of mouth today.
jokes.setup: Jerry mispronouncing a word during presentation
jokes.punchline: His attempt to explain it away with another malapropism
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jerry moment - the double malapropism escalates the humor perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I meant to say 'paradigm,' not 'parody-dime.' It's a common... um... misconstrual.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Did you just make up another word?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, I said it perfectly. You're all just not listening with your ear-holes.

jokes.text: Is it hot in here? I feel hot. Are you guys hot? How you guys doing? I'm good. You guys got any snacks?
jokes.setup: Carl meeting with Leslie and Tom
jokes.punchline: His scattered, unprofessional demeanor immediately apparent
jokes.explanation: Good character introduction for Carl's incompetence, sets up future jokes

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I've been thinking about the budget proposal, and I got some ideas, but first let me tell you about this dream I had where I was a pelican, and I could fly, but my wings kept getting tangled in spaghetti...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Carl, we really need to focus on the actual numbers here.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Right, right, yeah. So the numbers... they're like, really big, right? Some of them have commas in them. I don't trust commas. Who decided that commas go in numbers? It's weird.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Is he okay?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I'm great! I'm excellent. Hey, did you guys know that mayonnaise is an instrument? Wait, no, that's not right...

jokes.text: I wanted to read the book first, but then I realized there's no book version of Avatar.
jokes.setup: Carl talking about Avatar
jokes.punchline: His confusion about basic media formats
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Carl's confused thought process

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I watched Avatar on my phone. It was terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: All the blue people were squished.

jokes.text: I was wearing this t-shirt that said 'Kiss me, I'm Irish.' But no one would kiss me.
jokes.setup: Carl sharing St. Patrick's Day story
jokes.punchline: The sad reality of his failed attempt at holiday charm
jokes.explanation: Standard sad character moment, functional but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I tried to wear green on St. Patrick's Day once. My wife said I looked like a leprechaun that had given up on life.

jokes.text: Well, you might not be so confident once you've walked a mile in my size sevens. Kind of small feet. Actually, seven is the worldwide average. Boom!
jokes.setup: Carl trying to sound tough about his job
jokes.punchline: His defensive response about his small feet includes irrelevant statistics
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Carl's insecurity leading to weird flex about foot statistics

jokes.text: In the scenario you just laid out, you're the pervert. You understand that, right?
jokes.setup: Andy suggesting he put Mark in a headlock to demonstrate escape techniques
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact clarification of the problematic scenario
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron delivery - blunt logic applied to Andy's enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not going to put Mark in a headlock. That would give him the impression that I like him.

jokes.text: There was this one, How Far is Too Far Enough: The Teri Palliber Lonergan Story. This woman had agoraphobia and her therapist was obsessed with her, and he hid in her house. And then he attacked her and tried to eat her toes. Also, her daughter was having sex way too young.
jokes.setup: Donna explaining her knowledge from Lifetime movies
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd plot details delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with perfect Lifetime movie parody, Donna's casual delivery sells it

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I've watched a lot of Lifetime movies. I know things. Like, if a woman suddenly inherits a bakery in a small town right before Christmas, she's going to fall in love with the guy who runs the general store. He'll have a daughter from a previous relationship, and there will be a big misunderstanding at the Christmas pageant, but they'll work it out by the time they're decorating gingerbread houses together.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Also, if a woman books a spa weekend and the spa is actually a front for an international diamond smuggling ring run by her estranged twin sister, she's going to have to use her pilates instructor skills as a weapon.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: And if a woman wins a trip to an all-inclusive resort, the resort owner will turn out to be her college sweetheart who faked his own death to start a new life, and she'll have to decide between her current fiancé and this guy who's now also engaged to the resort's activities coordinator.

jokes.text: Oh! What's up now, mugger? Ann, that was awesome. That was really good.
jokes.setup: Ann successfully demonstrating escape technique
jokes.punchline: Andy's enthusiastic celebration and immediate pivot to encouragement
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment showing his supportive nature and simple enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That was amazing! You're like a ninja!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Thanks, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, now let's work on your upper body strength.

jokes.text: And after that, I'm going to show you this log I found. It's got, like, fifty worms on it. I call it 'worm log.'
jokes.setup: Carl discussing park patrol plans
jokes.punchline: His excited detour about a log full of worms with a literal name
jokes.explanation: Good Carl character moment - his childlike wonder about simple things

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I found this log full of worms and I named it Carl Junior because it's a smaller version of me.

jokes.text: When I was a kid, my parents used to make me hang out in the backyard a lot and just run around until I got tired. But if there's any criminals out there watching, I never get tired. And ladies, too.
jokes.setup: Carl describing his outdoor experience
jokes.punchline: The sad childhood story followed by awkward tough-guy posturing and inappropriate flirting
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment with Carl's oblivious oversharing and failed machismo

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I grew up in a very small town. My father left when I was three days old. My mother worked two jobs just to keep a roof over our heads. We didn't have much, but we had each other.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Now I'm basically a warrior. I can hunt, I can fish, I can fight. I'm very strong.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: You know, you have very nice skin. Has anyone ever told you that?

jokes.text: The first one got pushed into the creek by some kids. The second one, raccoons got onto. There was urine everywhere. And the third one was recently stolen. What's this one? This is the second one. The raccoon piss one.
jokes.setup: Carl explaining their equipment problems
jokes.punchline: The revelation they're currently using the urine-soaked cart
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with perfect gross-out punchline, good structure

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: We've been having some equipment problems at the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What kind of problems?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Well, our main equipment cart got damaged, so we've been using the backup cart.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The backup cart? That should be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that one got soaked in urine.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? How did that happen?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: A raccoon got into it last week. So now we're using the second backup cart.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, and what's wrong with that one?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: That's the urine-soaked one we're currently using.

jokes.text: Oh. That's Tom, probably. Are you serious? Tom, can you get off, please? Just run alongside the cart, okay?
jokes.setup: Cart struggling with weight
jokes.punchline: Immediate assumption it's Tom who needs to get off and run
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Tom's weakness, visual comedy of him running alongside

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Carl, this cart is too heavy. Someone needs to get off and run alongside.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I'll do it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no. Tom, you should probably—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Why me?

jokes.text: But just promise me you'll be ready, because, I mean, I don't want to destroy you.
jokes.setup: Andy about to be demonstrated on by Ron
jokes.punchline: His complete misunderstanding of who's in danger
jokes.explanation: Good Andy overconfidence moment, sets up the inevitable defeat

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Andy, I'm going to demonstrate a proper chokehold on you.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Ron, I don't think you should do that to me. You could really hurt yourself.

jokes.text: Andy. [implied unconscious/defeated state]
jokes.setup: Andy's confident challenge to Ron
jokes.punchline: Ron's calm stating of Andy's name after presumably destroying him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - minimal words, maximum impact. The understatement is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Andy.

jokes.text: With budget cuts, we can't afford a single safety light. There's been 10 assaults already this year. Wow. Really? Can't you station a Park Ranger out here? We have! Who do you think they're assaulting?
jokes.setup: Discussion about park safety and assault statistics
jokes.punchline: Rangers are the ones being assaulted, not preventing assaults
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy with perfect structure and unexpected revelation

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Did you know that park rangers are assaulted more often than any other federal employee?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: No, I didn't know that.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a huge problem. We need to do something about it.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Well, maybe they should stop assaulting people.

jokes.text: You guys have got to slow down. Can I just take a rest for a minute? No, Tom. Sorry. No can do. Sun's going down and it's real dangerous out here.
jokes.setup: Tom asking for rest during park patrol
jokes.punchline: Carl's urgent safety concern while Tom is clearly struggling
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Tom's fitness issues, Carl's oblivious urgency adds comedy

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Can we take a break? My feet are killing me.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: No breaks during park patrol, Tom. We need to stay vigilant.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Carl, come on. Just five minutes?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Tom, you're standing on a rattlesnake.

jokes.text: Sorry I squeezed your lights out there, son. No worries. Will you show me how to do that move, though?
jokes.setup: Ron apologizing for overpowering Andy
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately wants to learn the move that just defeated him
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's resilience and eagerness to learn

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm sorry. That was too rough.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, that was awesome! Teach me that move!

jokes.text: This is my co-worker, Jerry Gergich. Diabetic. Sloppily out of shape. Friend.
jokes.setup: Leslie introducing Jerry on TV
jokes.punchline: Her brutal honesty followed by the afterthought that he's a friend
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her brutal efficiency in describing Jerry with genuine affection as an afterthought

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is Jerry Gergich, our Parks Department deputy director. He's incompetent, accident-prone, and somehow manages to mess up the simplest tasks on a daily basis. But he's a good friend.

jokes.text: Who's next? Your frumpy uncle? Your simple neighbor? Your unpopular co-worker?
jokes.setup: Leslie making her case about park safety
jokes.punchline: Her examples are all descriptions of Jerry without realizing it
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie obliviousness - she's essentially describing Jerry repeatedly without awareness

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Look, park safety is a serious issue. We need to identify the hazards and eliminate them.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: For example, we have someone who's constantly tripping over things, breaking things, and creating a mess wherever they go.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We also have someone who's always making mistakes, wasting taxpayer money, and generally being a burden on the department.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And don't even get me started on the person who shows up late, leaves early, and somehow always manages to screw things up.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So if we can just remove these hazards from the park, I think we'll see a dramatic improvement in safety.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait... are you talking about me?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No! ...Actually, all three of those things are kind of you.

jokes.text: So... Make sure you bring the doofus who got his ass kicked. I don't know who you're referring to. We treat everyone with respect around here.
jokes.setup: Paul calling Jerry a doofus
jokes.punchline: Leslie's defensive response when she just described Jerry similarly on TV
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Leslie's inconsistency and protective instincts

dialogue.speaker: Paul
dialogue.line: You're being a doofus.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hey, you can't call him that. I call him that, but you can't.

jokes.text: He can't talk right now because he has hysterical muteness from trauma. Correct.
jokes.setup: Leslie covering for Jerry's reluctance to speak
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate confirmation of the fake condition
jokes.explanation: Good improvised cover-up with Jerry's helpful confirmation of the lie

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry has a very rare condition where he can't speak in public. It's called... um... Selective Mutism Disorder.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's right.

jokes.text: And Lord Sheldon... He lunged at a bird, I dropped the burrito, and it landed on a log in the creek. So, I go to reach for it, I lose my balance, and I fall on my shoulder really weird.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining what really happened
jokes.punchline: The increasingly pathetic chain of events leading to his injury
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry story - a series of small failures leading to injury over a breakfast burrito

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I was walking to my car, and I tripped over the curb. Then I fell into a ditch. Then a raccoon attacked me. Then I crawled out of the ditch, and a bus hit me. Then I crawled back into the ditch to get away from the bus, and that's when I broke my leg.

jokes.text: Damn, Jerry! You jumped in a creek for a burrito? What would you do for a Klondike Bar? Kill your wife?
jokes.setup: Tom's reaction to Jerry's burrito story
jokes.punchline: Escalating the commercial reference to murder
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Klondike commercial reference, validates Jerry's fear of mockery

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: A burrito? That's it? I once saw a guy eat a burrito so big, it required two hands and a forklift.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Not impossible. I was there. It was like that Old El Paso commercial where the girl is like 'Why not both?' Except the guy was like 'Why not all of them?' And then he died.

jokes.text: Oh! Hey! Ann Perkins, in the 'shoe-shouse.'
jokes.setup: Ann arriving at the shoeshine stand
jokes.punchline: Andy's made-up portmanteau word for Ann's location
jokes.explanation: Great Andy wordplay - his enthusiasm for making up words is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Shoe-shine stand! Or as I like to call it... the Shoe-Ann!

jokes.text: I got you one of those veggie muffins that you're always eating. Score. Yeah. It tastes like a rug. Shut up. Don't hit me.
jokes.setup: April brings Andy a muffin
jokes.punchline: Andy's honest reaction followed by immediate fear of retribution
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamic - April's rare kindness met with Andy's brutal honesty and fear

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This muffin is stale.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm sorry! Please don't hurt me!

jokes.text: Since when did he start doing stuff for other people? Now, I actually am worried that something happened to his brain.
jokes.setup: Andy showing kindness to April
jokes.punchline: Ann's genuine medical concern about Andy's personality change
jokes.explanation: Good character observation - Ann's medical training applied to Andy's rare kindness

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, I'm really concerned about you. You've been so nice to April lately. Have you been taking something? Should I be checking your pupils or running some blood work?

jokes.text: A citizen handed in a very interesting home video that I think the people of Pawnee will find very interesting.
jokes.setup: Carl threatening to reveal something on video
jokes.punchline: The repetition of 'interesting' suggests he's about to expose Jerry's lie
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for reveal, Carl's repetitive phrasing shows his limited vocabulary

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I have a video that proves you're lying.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm not lying!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Interesting. Interesting. Interesting.

jokes.text: There he is. This is my favorite part. Not willing to share with a dog.
jokes.setup: Carl showing video of Jerry's 'mugging'
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Jerry fell in creek trying to retrieve a burrito from his dog
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to Jerry's story with visual evidence, Carl's commentary adds humor

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: This is footage from the North Street intersection. As you can see, a man matching Jerry's description is being mugged.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's me!

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Wait, there's more footage from later that evening.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: The man, now identified as Jerry Gergich, is chasing his dog Pui-Pui near Ramsett Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: The dog has what appears to be a burrito in its mouth. Jerry attempts to retrieve it.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: And... he falls in the creek.

jokes.text: Fine. I'll have sex with you in exchange for the tape. That's not what I am saying. Okay?
jokes.setup: Leslie offering Carl a proposition
jokes.punchline: Her immediate jump to sexual negotiation
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie panic moment - her dedication to fixing problems leads to absurd offers

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Carl, I need a favor. What do you want in return?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know. What are you offering?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I could sleep with you.

jokes.text: I finally saw Avatar and I thought it lived up to the hype. Well, I'm not sure what that has to do with the mugging. Uh... Leslie. Counterpoint. I disagree with Carl. Okay. I have seen Avatar as well, and I think it exceeded the hype.
jokes.setup: Carl's promised bombshell revelation
jokes.punchline: His big reveal is just his Avatar opinion, followed by Leslie's desperate improvisation
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and subversion - Carl's 'bombshell' is trivial movie opinion, Leslie's panic response is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Avatar is overrated. The dialogue is wooden, the characters are one-dimensional, and the whole thing is just Dances with Wolves in space.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wow. Okay. That is... that is a controversial take. But you know what? I respect that. I think what Carl is saying is that we need to have more diverse opinions in Hollywood about big budget sci-fi films, and I for one think that's a conversation worth having.

jokes.text: Do you know that I bumped a cat that can stand up on its hinders for you? You disgust me, Knope. Get out of my sight. Yes, ma'am. Go. Go on. Oh, no, no, no. Don't make me chase you.
jokes.setup: Host angry about the Avatar segment
jokes.punchline: The absurd detail about bumping a cat act, followed by treating Leslie like a dog
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd escalation - the specific cat act detail and dog-like commands create surreal comedy

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: I'm furious about that Avatar segment! We bumped a cat act for that!

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: You know what? Treat her like a dog. Roll over!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay!

jokes.text: This is the only copy, and I am going to destroy it. Right after I watch it one more time. I'm sorry, but it is so good.
jokes.setup: Leslie promising to destroy the embarrassing video
jokes.punchline: Her immediate contradiction and admission she enjoys watching Jerry fail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - her good intentions immediately undermined by human curiosity

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to destroy this video immediately. I'm not going to watch it or enjoy it or... okay, I'm watching it again.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It never gets old. Jerry failing at basic human tasks.

jokes.text: We should just directly apply the food to your clothes.
jokes.setup: Jerry spilling his drink
jokes.punchline: Tom's suggestion to skip the eating step entirely
jokes.explanation: Great Tom observation about Jerry's consistent messiness with excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Just skip the eating step entirely. Go straight to the bathroom.

jokes.text: Because two more years until I retire with full benefits and pension. And the wife and I, we have bought a little cottage on a lake. And I am going to get myself a stack of mystery novels, a box of cigars, and I am going to sit back and enjoy my life.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why he can handle the mockery
jokes.punchline: His modest but genuinely happy retirement plans contrast with his office treatment
jokes.explanation: Beautiful Jerry character moment - his simple contentment makes the office mockery seem petty

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've thought about this a lot. When I retire, I'm going to have a nice little house downstate, maybe do some light woodworking, spend time with my family. And honestly? I'm gonna be happier than I've ever been.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So all those jokes, all those years of people making fun of me at this office? It doesn't matter. Because I'll be the one laughing when I'm living my best life and you're all still here dealing with each other.

jokes.text: Do you want the salmon or the 'twout'? Twout! Twout! Twout! Twout!
jokes.setup: April using Jerry's mispronunciation when asking about lunch
jokes.punchline: Everyone immediately latching onto the word for group mockery
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback ending - Jerry's earlier mistake becomes a permanent office meme

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Hey Jerry, where do you want to go for lunch? Some kind of... gazpacho?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Gazpacho!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Gazpacho!

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Gazpacho!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's gazpacho...

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Gazpacho.

tmdb_overview: After Jerry gets mugged by a bunch of kids, Leslie meets with the head park ranger in hopes of improving park safety. Meanwhile, everyone tries to be nicer to Jerry once he returns to the office.
insight: Safety hazard escalation drives 70 jokes in 24 minutes—Parks' densest episode yet.
